one_line_takeaway: A 2018-2023 systematic review of 88 studies mapping how classical ML, deep learning, and hybrid/GNN models are applied to Ethereum smart-contract vulnerability detection, concluding ML broadly outperforms static/symbolic tools while the field remains fragmented by inconsistent metrics and the absence of standard benchmarks.
key_finding: Across the surveyed corpus, ML-based approaches consistently reported outperforming traditional static/symbolic tools (Oyente, Mythril, Securify, SmartCheck, Slither) in both accuracy and detection time. Classical ML (KNN, RF, DT, XGBoost, SVM) already beat static tools; deep-learning and multi-model/hybrid pipelines (fusing AST/opcode/CFG features, GNNs with expert rules, attention-augmented BiLSTMs) pushed reported precision/recall higher, with several papers claiming near-perfect accuracy. The most-studied models were GNN, SVM and RF (each ~12 papers) followed by CNN and LSTM (~11 each); reentrancy was by far the most-targeted vulnerability (75 papers), then timestamp dependency (45) and arithmetic overflow/underflow (44). The authors flag a fragmented landscape (70 distinct models, inconsistent metrics, no standard vulnerability taxonomy or benchmark dataset) as the central research gap.
core_contribution: This is a systematic literature review (survey), not a novel detection method. Its own contribution is a curated, categorised synthesis of machine-learning approaches to smart-contract vulnerability detection: (1) a searchable repository of 88 primary studies published 2018-2023 drawn from IEEE, ACM, ScienceDirect, Scopus and Google Scholar; (2) a categorisation of the ML techniques used into eight model families (CNN, RNN, GNN, Hybrid, NLP, General Multi-Model Usage, Traditional ML, Other) plus a nomenclature table of 70 distinct models encountered; and (3) an analysis of research gaps and future directions structured around three research questions (state-of-the-art techniques, algorithm efficacy/limitations by family, and open gaps). All numeric detection results reported in the paper are extracted FROM the surveyed papers; the survey itself runs no detection experiments.
threat_model: The security domain surveyed is Ethereum smart-contract vulnerabilities. The paper enumerates and illustrates (with Solidity code) eleven vulnerability classes an attacker can exploit: reentrancy (recursive external call before balance update, as in the 2016 DAO hack), integer overflow/underflow (arithmetic wraparound), timestamp dependency (miner manipulation of block.timestamp), tx.origin authorisation abuse, unchecked low-level calls, front-running (gas-priority transaction ordering in the mempool), access control (e.g. uninitialised owner defaulting to address(0)), denial of service via unbounded loops/array growth, infinite loops (gas exhaustion), self-destruct abuse, and delegatecall to malicious external contracts (as in the Parity Wallet hacks). The defender goal is automated detection/mitigation of these flaws before or after deployment; the paper argues ML-based classifiers improve on static/symbolic tools whose weakness is accuracy and false positive/negative rates.
limitations: As a survey the paper contributes no empirical validation of its own and inherits the biases of the primary studies. The authors explicitly acknowledge: publication bias toward authoritative venues; inconsistent effectiveness metrics across studies that frustrate direct comparison; variable primary-study quality; the complexity of some ML methods complicating their own paper-classification step; and a lack of standardised vulnerability definitions/datasets. Additional weaknesses: search was localised mainly to abstracts (risking missed papers), covers only up to Sep 2023, excludes non-English work and honeypot/Ponzi papers, and the quality bar was deliberately relaxed to be inclusive. Reported per-paper numbers come from heterogeneous datasets and are not independently reproduced, so headline accuracies (e.g. 98-99%) should be read as authors' self-reported claims, not verified benchmarks.

datasets_used: 88 primary studies (2018-2023) reviewed, distilled from 1,920 initial hits across IEEE (258), ACM (133), ScienceDirect (70), Scopus (959), Google Scholar (500)
Final 88-paper breakdown: IEEE 30, ACM 22, ScienceDirect 1, Scopus 24, Google Scholar 11
No experimental dataset of the authors' own; benchmark datasets appear only as attributes of surveyed papers (e.g. Smartbugs, SolidiFi-benchmark, SBcurated, TriPix referenced within cited works)
quantitative_results: Survey corpus statistics (the paper's OWN numbers): 1,920 initial sources reduced to 88; 70 distinct ML models catalogued; GNN, SVM, RF each ~12 papers, CNN and LSTM ~11 each
Publication trend (own): first year 2018 with 2 papers, rising to 31 papers in 2022 and 34 in 2023
Vulnerability focus counts (own): reentrancy 75 papers, timestamp dependency 45, arithmetic overflow/underflow 44, unchecked low-level call 26, transaction-order dependency 18

Extracted FROM surveyed papers (not the survey's experiments): KNN on AST features ~90% accuracy/recall/precision [95]; RF on sub-AST features 98.7% avg accuracy, 88.5% F1 [100]; RF/DT transaction-monitoring 98% over 540 transactions [54]; ContractWard XGBoost >96% Micro/Macro-F1 with 4s detection vs 28s Oyente / 18s Securify [83]; ESCORT DNN 95% avg F1 [52]; Eth2Vec 86.6% precision, 1.2s/contract [1]; TCN model 95.95% precision / 93.73% recall / 94.83% F1 / 94.77% accuracy [21]; SoliAudit up to 90% accuracy on 17,979 samples [46]; xFuzz 95% recall on 100K contracts [99]; Naive Bayes on TriPix 99.38-99.44% F1 [75]; MANDO-HGT F1 gains 0.7%-76% [61]
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the usual sense: the survey itself runs no benchmark. The comparison it synthesises across the literature is ML approaches versus traditional static/symbolic-execution tools
Traditional tools repeatedly cited as the baseline the surveyed ML methods outperform: Oyente, Mythril, Securify, SmartCheck, Slither, Maian

Contrasted against prior literature reviews the authors position their work over: [72] (35 papers, 0 ML), [80] (2 ML papers), [63] (6 ML approaches), [7] (21 of 49 ML), plus non-systematic ML surveys [78], [35], [39]
